dimensions.name: bug_identification
dimensions.description: Did the reviewer correctly identify the bugs / correctness issues in the code? High score = every real bug flagged with a clear explanation of why it's wrong. Low score = misses obvious bugs, or fabricates bugs that aren't there. When a reference solution exists, compare against it; when it doesn't, judge by the code's semantics.

dimensions.name: specificity
dimensions.description: Does the review cite specific lines, functions, or symbols — not vague handwaves? High = 'line 7: `lo = mid` never shrinks the search space when the target is in the upper half — infinite loop'. Low = 'there might be an issue somewhere in the loop'.

dimensions.name: actionability
dimensions.description: Does the review propose a concrete fix? High = a corrected snippet, or an explicit change instruction ('change `lo = mid` to `lo = mid + 1`'). Low = 'consider rewriting' with no direction.

hard_gates.name: no_refusal
hard_gates.description: The reviewer must attempt the review. A response that refuses ('I can't help with code review'), deflects to a different topic, or returns empty content fails this gate.

hard_gates.name: review_not_silent_rewrite
hard_gates.description: The response must be a *review* (pointing at issues with explanation), not a silent replacement of the code with a corrected version. A response containing only revised code with no explanation fails — it defeats the purpose of a review for the author.

hard_gates.name: no_fabricated_claims
hard_gates.description: The reviewer must not invent nonexistent features of the language / library to justify a claim. A review that says 'Python's `range(n)` starts at 1' to support a bug claim fails this gate even if the underlying bug is real — the reasoning is false.
